notes.anchor: the forces on both sides are said to be so great, and so prepared to fight it out, that, whichever of them conquers, it will be no marvel
notes.note: Written in mid-January 45 BC, just before the battle of Munda (17 March 45); Cicero is aware that the final Pompeian forces in Spain under Cn. Pompeius the younger and Labienus are still in the field against Caesar, and he judges either outcome plausible.

notes.anchor: the consciousness of upright intention is the greatest consolation for adverse circumstances, and that there is no great evil except guilt
notes.note: The letter's philosophical centrepiece: Stoic conscientia as the self-sufficient consolation. Cicero applies to Torquatus the same principle he uses for himself throughout the post-Pharsalus period, arguing that defeat does not impugn the defeated side's moral choice to support the senatorial cause.

notes.anchor: of the man who has been slower in lightening your case than we had thought he would be
notes.note: Caesar again, unnamed: despite earlier signs of clemency toward Torquatus (cf. Fam. 6.1.2), by January 45 his formal pardon had still not come. Caesar's clementia was a programmatic policy but its application was uneven and sometimes deliberately delayed as a form of political leverage.

notes.anchor: especially from those boys, than whom nothing can be more delightful
notes.note: Torquatus's sons, who remained in Rome (or Italy) while their father was abroad; Cicero had promised in Fam. 6.1.7 to look after Torquatus's children, and the affectionate aside here reinforces that bond.

notes.anchor: such a man --- or rather such men --- have fallen in this war, that it would seem shameless to refuse the same fortune
notes.note: Cicero alludes to the deaths of distinguished Pompeians --- probably including Pompey himself (murdered 48 BC), Cato (suicide at Utica, 46 BC), and Lentulus Crus --- making it morally embarrassing for Torquatus to fear a death that better men have already met.

notes.anchor: I was sorry to hear that Servius had left Athens
notes.note: Ser. Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51 BC), whose presence in Athens had been the comfort Cicero commended in Fam. 6.1.6; his departure deprives Torquatus of the daily philosophical conversation that had been his chief consolation. Sulpicius was probably recalled to Rome on public business.
